sound_type: rapid chattering chirp -- a fast, aggressive, sustained chattering vocalization, the 69th acoustic element, faster and more sustained than the Short-tailed Weasel's bark/chitter, a machine-gun burst of high-pitched aggression from the open woodland
ecosystem: open woodland -- scattered trees with grassy or shrubby understory, parkland, savanna-like environments, agricultural margins, open forests with good ground visibility, the sixth consecutive new habitat in the Mustelidae sextet
season: year-round resident throughout Washington State, from lowlands to mid-elevation
species_pair: Short-tailed Weasel (SPS-067) -- the weasel pair, consecutive weasels, smaller then larger, edge then woodland, bark then chatter

coordinates.description: Reference for open woodland habitat in the southern Puget Sound lowlands. Long-tailed Weasels are widespread across Washington from lowlands to mid-elevation, inhabiting open woodlands, meadows, grasslands, and agricultural margins. Vocalizations most commonly heard during territorial encounters and aggressive confrontations.

taxonomy_note.description: Ninth mammalian entry. Seventh mammalian species. Seventh Carnivora, sixth Mustelidae. SIXTH CONSECUTIVE MUSTELIDAE -- the Mustelidae SEXTET (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66, Short-tailed Weasel 67, Long-tailed Weasel 68). The LONGEST CONSECUTIVE FAMILY STREAK IN ENGINE HISTORY. Neogale frenata -- reclassified from Mustela to Neogale based on molecular phylogenetics, placing it in the SAME GENUS as the Mink (Neogale vison, degree 64). The weasel and the mink are closer relatives than the Long-tailed and Short-tailed Weasels.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Neogale from Greek neos (new) + gale (weasel), meaning 'new weasel.' Same genus as the Mink (Neogale vison). frenata from Latin frenatum (bridled), referring to the facial markings -- the yellowish wash on the face suggesting a bridle-like pattern. Common name 'Long-tailed Weasel' distinguishes it from the Short-tailed Weasel (Mustela erminea).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Neogale -- the same genus as the Mink (Neogale vison, degree 64). Two Neogale in the sextet, separated by four degrees (64 and 68). The Long-tailed Weasel was historically in Mustela but was reclassified based on molecular evidence showing it is more closely related to the Mink than to the Short-tailed Weasel. The genus pair (Neogale at 64 and 68) is the only repeated genus in the sextet.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- seventh entry. All seven mammalian Carnivora entries are Mustelidae (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66, Short-tailed Weasel 67, Long-tailed Weasel 68, plus the Spotted Owl's predatory role at degree 4). The order's entire mammalian representation comes from a single family. No non-mustelid mammalian carnivore has entered the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- ninth mammalian entry. Seventh mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 13.0% (9 of 69 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-68) is now eight degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Mustelidae's sixth entry at degree 68. The SEXTET: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66), Short-tailed Weasel (67), Long-tailed Weasel (68). Six consecutive entries -- the LONGEST CONSECUTIVE FAMILY STREAK IN ENGINE HISTORY, surpassing the Swallow Quintet (Hirundinidae 26-30). Mustelidae now represents 6 of 8 PNW mustelid species (75% coverage). Three-quarters of Washington's mustelid diversity documented.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Variable, estimated 1,800-7,000 Hz for the chattering chirp. Slightly lower fundamental frequency than the Short-tailed Weasel due to larger body, but the difference is subtle. Both species produce vocalizations in overlapping frequency ranges.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2,500-5,500 (the band most characteristic of the rapid chattering quality)
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song but a RAPID CHATTERING CHIRP. A fast, aggressive, sustained vocalization produced during territorial disputes, predator confrontations, and aggressive encounters. Additional vocalizations: squealing (distress), hissing (close-range defense), trilling (mating interactions, rare). The chattering chirp dominates -- the sound of sustained aggression and territorial confrontation.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual chirps: 0.05-0.15 seconds. Chattering sequences: 2-10 seconds. Total vocal events during encounters may last 15-90 seconds, longer than the Short-tailed Weasel's typical bout.
song_metrics.note_structure: RAPID CHATTERING: Continuous stream of high-pitched chirping notes delivered at a faster rate than the Short-tailed Weasel's bark/chitter pattern. The chattering is more sustained and less percussive -- a machine-gun burst rather than a cannon volley.
song_metrics.note_types: Chattering chirp (rapid, sustained, aggressive -- dominant and diagnostic), squeal (distress, high-pitched sustained), hiss (close-range defense), trill (mating contact, soft, rare). The chattering chirp is the Long-tailed Weasel's signature.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Sustained chattering bouts. Less bark-dominated than the Short-tailed Weasel's bark-chitter combinations. The vocal display is a continuous stream of aggressive sound rather than punctuated explosions.
song_metrics.timbre: Aggressive, high-pitched, sustained. Slightly fuller than the Short-tailed Weasel's shrill bark/chitter due to the larger body and vocal apparatus. More relentless and confrontational in character, less frantic and explosive.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Same energy rating as the Short-tailed Weasel but with a different delivery character. The Long-tailed Weasel's E=4 is sustained confrontation; the Short-tailed Weasel's E=4 is explosive desperation. Same energy, different strategy.
song_metrics.tempo: Very fast. Faster repetition rate than the Short-tailed Weasel. The Long-tailed Weasel compensates for the fractionally lower pitch with more chirps per second, creating a denser acoustic texture.
song_metrics.context: AGGRESSIVE/TERRITORIAL. Produced during territorial disputes, predator confrontations, encounters with conspecifics, and defense of cached prey. The Long-tailed Weasel is bolder than the Short-tailed Weasel and vocalizes more readily in confrontational situations. Historical accounts describe Long-tailed Weasels chattering aggressively at dogs, cats, and humans.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in vocalizations. The species is widespread and acoustically consistent across its range.
song_metrics.key_property: THE RAPID CHATTERING CHIRP -- the 69th acoustic element. Fast, aggressive, sustained. E=4. A wall of sound rather than a volley. More continuous and confrontational than the Short-tailed Weasel's bark/chitter. The larger weasel's bolder, more sustained vocal challenge.

physical_description.overall: Medium-small elongated mustelid, 280-420 mm total length, 200-450 g. Larger than the Short-tailed Weasel (150-350 g) by roughly 30-50%. Extreme sexual dimorphism -- males roughly twice the weight of females. Elongated, tubular body adapted for burrow pursuit. Distinguished from the Short-tailed Weasel by longer tail, larger overall size, and yellowish ventral wash.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 68, the diagnostic features are: (1) LONGER TAIL -- approximately 40-70% of head-body length, much longer proportionally than the Short-tailed Weasel. (2) LARGER BODY -- 200-450 g vs. 150-350 g. (3) YELLOWISH ventral coloration extending onto the face. (4) BLACK TAIL TIP -- year-round, as in the Short-tailed Weasel. (5) SUMMER: brown to cinnamon-brown dorsal with yellowish-white underparts. (6) WINTER: may turn white in northern/high-elevation populations.
physical_description.fur_detail: SUMMER: Rich brown to cinnamon-brown dorsal surface with pale yellow to cream-white underparts. The ventral coloration extends onto the face as a distinctive yellowish wash, more extensive than the Short-tailed Weasel's clean white underparts. Brown cap pattern with pale cheeks and chin. WINTER: In northern populations and at higher elevations, turns white except for the black tail tip. In Puget Sound lowlands, typically remains brown or shows only partial whitening. The photoperiod trigger is the same mechanism as the Short-tailed Weasel.
physical_description.dental_detail: Carnassial teeth typical of Carnivora. 34 teeth total. Strong jaw relative to body size, slightly more robust than the Short-tailed Weasel. The killing technique is the mustelid standard: cervical bite to the base of the skull. Effective against prey up to several times the weasel's own body weight.
physical_description.field_id: In open woodland: a small, elongated brown (summer) or white (winter) animal with short legs and a notably LONG tail with a black tip. Distinguished from the Short-tailed Weasel by: longer tail (the most reliable field distinction), larger overall size, yellowish wash on the underparts and face (summer). Movement is rapid, low to the ground, with a characteristic bounding lope. More commonly seen in open habitats than the Short-tailed Weasel, which prefers denser cover.

nodes.title: The Mustelidae Sextet -- Six Consecutive (63-64-65-66-67-68)
nodes.description: Six consecutive Mustelidae: Otter (river/estuary, 5-14 kg, social) -> Mink (creek bank, 0.5-1.5 kg, solitary) -> Marten (old-growth canopy, 0.5-1.2 kg, arboreal) -> Fisher (dense forest, 2-6 kg, terrestrial) -> Short-tailed Weasel (meadow-forest edge, 150-350 g, terrestrial) -> Long-tailed Weasel (open woodland, 200-450 g, terrestrial). The LONGEST CONSECUTIVE FAMILY STREAK IN ENGINE HISTORY. Six species, six habitats, six sounds. Surpasses the Swallow Quintet (26-30).

nodes.title: The Rapid Chattering Chirp -- 69th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Rapid, aggressive, sustained chattering chirp. E=4. Faster and more sustained than the Short-tailed Weasel's bark/chitter (degree 67). A machine-gun burst of high-pitched aggression from the open woodland. The larger body produces a slightly lower fundamental frequency but compensates with faster repetition rate. A wall of sound rather than a volley.

nodes.title: The Weasel Pair (67-68) -- Third Within-Family Species Pair
nodes.description: Short-tailed Weasel (67) then Long-tailed Weasel (68). The engine's third within-family species pair (after Flicker 21-22 and Beaver 61-62). Smaller then larger. Edge then woodland. Bark then chatter. Different genera (Mustela vs. Neogale). The pair within the sextet. Each pair follows the same pattern: two closely related species in consecutive degrees, differentiated by size, habitat, and acoustic character.

nodes.title: Six Habitats, Six Species -- The Complete Gradient
nodes.description: River (63) -> creek bank (64) -> old-growth canopy (65) -> dense forest (66) -> meadow-forest edge (67) -> open woodland (68). Six consecutive Mustelidae, six different habitats. No two sextet members share a primary habitat. The gradient traces a complete arc from deep water to open terrestrial habitat. Open woodland is the sextet's most open position -- the endpoint of a journey from the river.

nodes.title: Open Woodland -- Most Open Habitat in the Sextet
nodes.description: Scattered trees with grassy or shrubby understory. Parkland. Savanna-like environments. The Long-tailed Weasel prefers more open habitat than any other mustelid in the sextet. Where the Short-tailed Weasel hunts at the forest edge, the Long-tailed Weasel hunts in the open spaces between trees. Habitat partitioning with the Short-tailed Weasel: larger species takes the open ground, smaller species retreats to denser cover.

nodes.title: The Neogale Connection -- Same Genus as Mink
nodes.description: Long-tailed Weasel (Neogale frenata) and Mink (Neogale vison, degree 64) share a genus. Two Neogale in the sextet, separated by four degrees. The reclassification reveals that the Long-tailed Weasel is more closely related to the Mink than to the Short-tailed Weasel (Mustela erminea), despite sharing the common name 'weasel.' Common names deceive; molecular phylogenetics clarifies. The only repeated genus in the sextet.

nodes.title: Third Surplus Killer -- Family-Level Pattern
nodes.description: Mink (64, opportunity-driven), Short-tailed Weasel (67, metabolic necessity), Long-tailed Weasel (68, aggressive generalism). Three of six sextet members exhibit surplus killing. Half the family sequence. Surplus killing is a Mustelidae family trait, not a species-specific anomaly. The Long-tailed Weasel's surplus killing is driven by aggressive generalist predation -- kill when opportunity presents, cache against future scarcity.

nodes.title: Weasel Pair Acoustic Comparison (67 vs. 68)
nodes.description: Both E=4. The Short-tailed Weasel's bark/chitter is percussive, explosive, frantic -- single shots and bursts. The Long-tailed Weasel's chattering chirp is sustained, relentless, confrontational -- a continuous stream. Same energy, different delivery. The Short-tailed Weasel sounds desperate; the Long-tailed Weasel sounds bold. Both are disproportionately loud for their body size.

nodes.title: Five Genera, Six Entries -- The Neogale Pair
nodes.description: Lontra (63), Neogale (64, 68), Martes (65), Pekania (66), Mustela (67). Five genera across six entries. Four reclassified (Otter, Mink, Fisher, Long-tailed Weasel), two original (Marten, Short-tailed Weasel). Neogale is the only repeated genus. The weasel pair (67-68) spans two different genera (Mustela, Neogale) -- the consecutive weasels are in different genera while the Mink and Long-tailed Weasel (four degrees apart) share one.

nodes.title: The Sam Beam / Iron & Wine Parallel
nodes.description: Maximum contrast. Sam Beam's whispered folk (E=2, Sub Pop) paired with the Long-tailed Weasel's aggressive chattering chirp (E=4). Both operate at extremes of their register. The whisper is not shy; the chatter is not rage. Both are genuine expressions of their nature. The most extreme energy contrast in a Part A/Part B pair.

nodes.title: Seventh Carnivore -- Still All Mustelidae
nodes.description: Carnivora now has seven entries, all from Mustelidae: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66), Short-tailed Weasel (67), Long-tailed Weasel (68). The order's entire mammalian representation comes from a single family. Seven entries without a non-mustelid carnivore. When a canid, felid, ursid, or raptor eventually enters the engine, it will be Carnivora's first non-mustelid representative.

nodes.title: Eighth Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degrees 61-68: eight consecutive original CSV species. The longest original-CSV streak in engine history extends yet again. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered. The CSV's mammalian sequence remains clean and uncontested.

nodes.title: Ninth Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era Extends
nodes.description: Nine mammalian entries across eight degrees (61-68). Seven mammalian species: Beaver, River Otter, Mink, American Marten, Fisher, Short-tailed Weasel, Long-tailed Weasel. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae). Mammalian representation rises to 13.0% (9 of 69 entries). The mammalian era is eight degrees deep and increasingly imbalanced.

nodes.title: Retrospective Override Pattern -- Second Consecutive
nodes.description: The retrospective at degree 66 declared four consecutive Mustelidae 'non-negotiable.' Degree 67 was a fifth. The retrospective at degree 67 declared five consecutive Mustelidae 'non-negotiable.' Degree 68 is a sixth. Two consecutive overrides. The retrospective's recommendations about species selection are advisory -- the CSV is authoritative. The system's predictive function regarding the CSV is broken.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE WEASELS. The weasel pair. Smaller (150-350 g) vs. larger (200-450 g). Meadow-forest edge vs. open woodland. Shrill bark/chitter vs. rapid chattering chirp. Different genera (Mustela vs. Neogale). The pair within the sextet. Two weasels, two habitats, two acoustic strategies, two genera.

SAME GENUS (Neogale). Creek bank vs. open woodland. Both fierce, both surplus killers. The Neogale genus pair -- the only repeated genus in the sextet. The reclassification reveals the weasel and the mink are closer relatives than the two 'weasels' (Long-tailed and Short-tailed).

SEXTET CONTINUES. Dense forest vs. open woodland. Specialist vs. generalist. The Fisher retreats into dense forest; the Long-tailed Weasel steps into open space. Opposite ends of the habitat-openness spectrum among terrestrial mustelids.

SEXTET BOOKENDS. River/estuary (Otter, 63) vs. open woodland (Long-tailed Weasel, 68). Social vs. solitary. 5-14 kg vs. 200-450 g. The sextet spans water to open land, 30x size difference, gregarious to solitary.
BOTH FORMERLY MUSTELA-ADJACENT. Canopy vs. ground. Old-growth specialist vs. open-habitat generalist. The arboreal and terrestrial extremes of small-bodied mustelid ecology in the sextet.
Ninth mammalian entry. Seventh mammalian species. Seventh Carnivora, sixth Mustelidae. Mammalian representation rises to 13.0% (9 of 69 entries). Two orders, three families, seven species.

Open woodland habitat. Third surplus killer in the sextet. Aggressive generalist predation. Habitat partitioning with sympatric Short-tailed Weasel. Rodent population control through bold, confrontational hunting.
